title: WAGNER-MEERWEIN REARRANGEMENT
summary: Overview page for the Wagner-Meerwein rearrangement describing carbocation rearrangements via 1,2-shifts, including protic- and Lewis-acid-triggered skeletal reorganizations and competing alkene or capture pathways.
notes: Emphasizes skeletal rearrangement through carbocation intermediates, structural reorganization by hydride/alkyl/aryl shifts, and stereochemical consequences of migration.

title: WAGNER-MEERWEIN REARRANGEMENT
summary: Application page for the Wagner-Meerwein rearrangement highlighting acid- or epoxide-triggered 1,2-shifts in the syntheses of KW-2189, hydroxyfenchone, sesquiterpene ketone frameworks, and quadrone.
notes: Examples show rearrangements from secondary or tertiary carbocations, often initiated by dehydration, epoxidation, or strong Brønsted acid treatment.

title: WEINREB KETONE SYNTHESIS
summary: Overview page for Weinreb ketone synthesis describing conversion of activated carboxylic acid derivatives into N-methoxy-N-methylamides and their controlled reaction with organometallic reagents to furnish ketones or aldehydes.
notes: Covers preparation and reactivity of Weinreb amides, single-addition control through chelation, and reduction pathways from esters or lactones.

title: WEINREB KETONE SYNTHESIS
summary: Application page for Weinreb ketone synthesis featuring ketone installation in the syntheses of tuberostemonine, tautomycin fragments, nicandrenones, and dictamnol.
notes: Examples emphasize late-stage conversion of lactones or alkynyl/alkenyl precursors into Weinreb amides followed by chemoselective organometallic addition.

title: WHARTON FRAGMENTATION
summary: Overview page for the Wharton fragmentation describing base-induced fragmentation of cyclic or acyclic 1,3-diol monosulfonates or related substrates to medium-sized cyclic alkenes and unsaturated fragments.
notes: Highlights anti stereoelectronic requirements, relation to Grob-type fragmentations, and substrate-dependent side-product formation such as oxetanes.

title: WHARTON FRAGMENTATION
summary: Application page for Wharton fragmentation showcasing its use in xenicane synthesis, CP-263,114 core construction, taxane framework assembly, and substituted kainic acid synthesis.
notes: Examples use mesylate or tosylate precursors and strong base to trigger fragmentation into medium-ring or highly functionalized unsaturated products.

title: WHARTON OLEFIN SYNTHESIS (WHARTON TRANSPOSITION)
summary: Overview page for the Wharton olefin synthesis or Wharton transposition describing conversion of α,β-epoxyketones into allylic alcohols via hydrazone formation and nitrogen extrusion.
notes: Contrasts the Wharton transposition with Kishner eliminative reduction and emphasizes hydrazine-mediated reduction of epoxyketones to allylic alcohols.

title: WHARTON OLEFIN SYNTHESIS (WHARTON TRANSPOSITION)
summary: Application page for the Wharton olefin synthesis highlighting allylic alcohol formation in the syntheses of OSW-1 precursors, decipienin A, warburganal, and physoperuvine.
notes: Examples feature hydrazine-mediated transposition after epoxidation of enones and subsequent oxidation or elaboration of the allylic alcohol products.

title: WILLIAMSON ETHER SYNTHESIS
summary: Overview page for the Williamson ether synthesis describing SN2 reaction of alkoxides or phenoxides with alkyl halides or sulfonates to form dialkyl or aryl alkyl ethers.
notes: Summarizes alkoxide generation, substrate scope, leaving-group trends, and limitations such as elimination or C-alkylation with hindered or activated aryl systems.

title: WILLIAMSON ETHER SYNTHESIS
summary: Application page for the Williamson ether synthesis featuring phase-transfer and intramolecular etherifications in the syntheses of methanophenazine, asimicin, fumagillol, and archaeal macrocyclic diether lipids.
notes: Examples include intermolecular and intramolecular substitutions of mesylates or tosylates, often under pyridine, DMSO, or phase-transfer conditions.
